Guideline: Use channels to connect multi-stage processing pipelines
Description: Build data pipelines by connecting stages with channels, where each stage is a goroutine that reads from an input channel and writes to an output channel
Do: Compose pipeline stages as functions that accept and return channels with proper context cancellation
Dont: Use shared mutable state or direct function calls between processing stages

Guideline: Distribute work across multiple goroutines
Description: Start multiple goroutines reading from the same channel to parallelize CPU-bound or I/O-bound work across available cores
Do: Launch a fixed number of worker goroutines consuming from a shared work channel
Dont: Spawn an unbounded number of goroutines per item which can exhaust memory and file descriptors

Guideline: Merge multiple channels into a single output channel
Description: Use a fan-in function to merge results from multiple concurrent producers into a single channel for downstream consumption
Do: Use sync.WaitGroup to track completion and close the merged output channel when all inputs are done
Dont: Read from multiple channels in a loop without proper synchronization or close handling

Guideline: Use buffered channels to control pipeline flow
Description: Apply backpressure by using buffered channels so that fast producers slow down when consumers cannot keep up, preventing memory exhaustion
Do: Size channel buffers based on measured throughput and memory constraints
Dont: Use unbounded queues or ever-growing slices that consume unlimited memory under load

Guideline: Use sarama consumer groups for reliable Kafka consumption
Description: Use IBM/sarama ConsumerGroup for partition assignment, rebalancing, and offset management in Go Kafka consumers
Do: Implement the ConsumerGroupHandler interface with Setup Cleanup and ConsumeClaim methods
Dont: Use manual partition consumers without consumer group coordination for multi-instance deployments

Guideline: Use async producer with error handling for throughput
Description: Use sarama AsyncProducer for high-throughput message production with proper error and success channel handling
Do: Drain both Errors and Successes channels from the async producer to prevent goroutine leaks
Dont: Ignore the Errors channel or use sync producer for high-throughput workloads

Guideline: Use NATS JetStream for persistent messaging
Description: Use NATS JetStream for durable, at-least-once message delivery with consumer acknowledgments and replay capabilities
Do: Create durable consumers with explicit acknowledgment and configure stream retention policies
Dont: Use core NATS without JetStream for workloads requiring delivery guarantees

Guideline: Accumulate items and flush in batches
Description: Collect individual items into batches based on size or time thresholds before processing, reducing per-item overhead for I/O operations
Do: Flush batches when either the batch size limit or a time deadline is reached whichever comes first
Dont: Process items one at a time for bulk operations or accumulate without a time-based flush deadline

Guideline: Use a token bucket or ticker for rate-limited processing
Description: Apply rate limiting to pipeline stages that interact with external APIs or rate-limited resources to avoid throttling or bans
Do: Use golang.org/x/time/rate Limiter to control throughput at a configurable rate
Dont: Process items as fast as possible without rate limiting when calling rate-limited external services

Guideline: Finish in-flight work before shutting down pipeline
Description: On shutdown signal, stop accepting new items, drain remaining items from channels, and wait for all workers to complete
Do: Use context cancellation to stop producers and sync.WaitGroup to wait for workers to finish in-flight items
Dont: Terminate the process immediately losing in-flight data that has been read from the source but not yet processed

Guideline: Use a separate channel for error handling in pipelines
Description: Route errors to a dedicated channel so that the main data path stays clean and errors can be handled centrally by a dedicated goroutine
Do: Send errors to a separate buffered error channel and aggregate them in a dedicated error handler
Dont: Mix errors into the data channel or silently drop errors within pipeline stages

Guideline: Route failed messages to a dead letter topic for later analysis
Description: Messages that fail processing after all retries should be sent to a dead letter queue for investigation, replay, or manual processing
Do: Send permanently failed messages to a DLQ with original metadata and error details preserved
Dont: Discard messages that fail processing or retry indefinitely blocking the pipeline

Guideline: Track pipeline throughput and latency with Prometheus
Description: Instrument pipeline stages with Prometheus counters, histograms, and gauges to monitor throughput, latency, backlog size, and error rates
Do: Register per-stage metrics for items processed and processing duration and current queue depth
Dont: Rely solely on logs to understand pipeline performance without quantitative metrics

Guideline: Retry transient errors with exponential backoff and jitter
Description: Use exponential backoff with jitter for retrying transient errors like network timeouts or rate limits, with a maximum retry count
Do: Use a backoff library with configurable max retries and exponential delay with jitter
Dont: Retry immediately in a tight loop or use fixed delays without jitter causing thundering herd

Guideline: Use context to propagate cancellation through the pipeline
Description: Pass context.Context through every pipeline stage so that cancellation at the top level propagates to all goroutines, enabling clean shutdown
Do: Accept context as the first parameter in every pipeline stage and check ctx.Done() in select statements
Dont: Use global stop channels or boolean flags for cancellation instead of the standard context package

Guideline: Use a fixed-size worker pool with bounded concurrency
Description: Create a pool of worker goroutines reading from a shared job channel to limit concurrency, control resource usage, and maintain predictable throughput
Do: Size the worker pool based on the bottleneck resource and use a semaphore or fixed goroutine count
Dont: Create a goroutine per task or use an unbounded pool that grows without limit

Guideline: Use sync.Pool to reuse allocations in hot paths
Description: Use sync.Pool to recycle frequently allocated objects like byte buffers, reducing garbage collection pressure in high-throughput pipelines
Do: Pool objects that are expensive to allocate and used frequently such as byte slices and struct buffers
Dont: Allocate new buffers on every iteration in a tight processing loop causing GC pressure

Guideline: Track processing offsets for resumable pipelines
Description: Persist the last successfully processed offset or position so the pipeline can resume from where it left off after a restart
Do: Commit offsets after successful processing and load the last offset on startup
Dont: Process items without tracking progress losing all work on restart

Guideline: Use Protocol Buffers for high-throughput internal serialization
Description: Use protobuf for internal service-to-service and pipeline serialization for smaller payloads, faster encoding/decoding, and schema evolution support
Do: Use protobuf for internal pipeline data and JSON only for external API boundaries
Dont: Use JSON encoding for high-volume internal pipeline messages where performance matters

Guideline: Write integration tests for complete pipeline flows
Description: Test the entire pipeline from input to output using real or containerized dependencies to verify correctness under realistic conditions
Do: Use testcontainers-go to spin up real Kafka or database instances for integration tests
Dont: Test only individual functions in isolation without ever testing the complete pipeline flow
